summary: Stancil argues that dbt made data modeling easy but not simple, creating a generation of “analytics pretendgineers” who can write models but lack the architectural discipline to build durable data programs. He contends that the fix isn’t more features or education, but a framework with strong, opinionated guardrails—“dbt on Rails”—that enforces structure and prevents chaotic model sprawl.
money_quotes: dbt changed all this—mostly for the better, but a bit for the worse.
A hard thing became an easy thing.
In other words, dbt’s explosive popularity turned a generation of data analysts into analytics pretendgineers.
Behaviors are shaped by our environment and what that environment lets us do, not the other way around.
That’s what I want for dbt—either built into dbt Labs, or on top of it, like Django is on Python.
key_insight: dbt lowered the barrier to building data models but, without enforced structure, it also lowered the quality of data-program design, so the real fix is opinionated frameworks rather than more tooling or training.
